Molds of at least seven individuals; the original shell material doesn't seem to be preserved
Measurement 6-7mm
An outline can be seen, as well as the (dorsal?) ribs.
Ventral view. The aperture is visible as well as the three ribs that characterize the species; a wide central one and a slimmer one on either side
An outline can be seen, as well as faint ribs. Dorsal view
Dorsal view at an angle, so that the curvature at the aperture is visible
Many of the individuals are not well-preserved, with minimal details visible

Stem fragment of crinoid - Star-shaped axial canal visible in the middle of the cross-section
Stem fragment of crinoid - Star-shaped axial canal visible in the middle of the cross-section. The asymmetry suggests that the fossil has undergone compression.
Stem fragment of crinoid - It appears to have been almost flattened by compression.

Orthoceratoid - 3-4cm. The chamber walls are faintly visible.

Orthoceratoid - 3-4cm. Cross-section. The siphuncle and the convex wall of the chamber can be seen

Orthoceratoid - ~3cm. Cross-section. The siphuncle can be faintly seen.

Orthoceratoid - ~3cm. The chamber walls can be seen, with slightly wavy sutures.
Orthoceratoid - ~3cm. The fracture reveals the wavy sutures between the chambers very clearly.
Orthoceratoid - ~3cm. Its tip doesn't seem to have been broken.

Dnipro River, Kamianske
Cut and polished face - the enamel plates form pale loops; scale in centimetres. cf. Mammuthus primigenius.
Oblique view - the enamel bands run through the full thickness of the piece
Cut surface beside the natural one - the unworked tooth surface shows along the top
Top view of the polished face - the characteristic zig-zag pattern of the plates
Detail of the enamel loops - the parallel ridges that ground plant food
